news_summary: Today's space news highlights inspiring achievements in human space exploration with the recognition of rover challenge winners, the successful preparation of Artemis II astronauts, and a groundbreaking discovery of a comet reversing its spin direction.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des réalisations inspirantes dans le domaine de l'exploration spatiale humaine, avec la remise des prix aux lauréats du Rover Challenge, la préparation réussie des astronautes d'Artemis II et une découverte révolutionnaire concernant une comète qui inverse le sens de sa rotation.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan logros inspiradores en la exploración espacial tripulada, con el reconocimiento a los ganadores del desafío de los rovers, la preparación satisfactoria de los astronautas de Artemis II y un descubrimiento revolucionario sobre un cometa que invierte el sentido de su rotación.

articles.title: NASA Announces 32nd Annual Human Exploration Rover Challenge Winners
articles.summary: NASA’s 32nd annual Human Exploration Rover Challenge, one of the agency’s longest-standing student challenges, culminated April 10-11 with its final excursion event at the U.S. Space & Rocket Center near NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. Spanning nine months, the challenge tasks student teams from around the world to design, build, and test […]
articles.content:
NASA’s 32nd annual Human Exploration Rover Challenge, one of the agency’s longest-standing student challenges, culminated April 10-11 with its final excursion event at the U.S. Space & Rocket Center near NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama.

Spanning nine months, the challenge tasks student teams from around the world to design, build, and test a lunar rover powered by either human pilots or remote control. The annual competition concluded with an awards ceremony recognizing the top-performing teams.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that

supports HTML5 video This video highlights student teams from around the world that competed in NASA’s 2026 Human Exploration Rover Challenge, held April 10–11 at the U.S. Space & Rocket Center near the agency’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. Teams navigated a challenging obstacle course and completed complex mission tasks using human-powered and remote-controlled rovers. NASA

In the human-powered division, Parish Episcopal School in Dallas, Texas earned first place in the high school division, while the University of Central Missouri in Warrensburg, Missouri, won the college and university title. In the remote-control division, Gould Academy in Bethel, Maine, earned the top award in the middle and high school division, and The University of Alabama in Huntsville in Huntsville, Alabama, secured the college and university title.

More than 500 students representing 42 teams from around the world participated in the 32nd annual competition. Teams included students from 28 colleges and universities, 13 high schools, and one middle school across 18 U.S. states, Puerto Rico,

Teams were scored on their ability to navigate a half-mile obstacle course, complete mission-specific task challenges, and pass multiple safety and design reviews conducted by NASA engineers, with awards presented across human-powered and remote-control divisions.

“This challenge gives students a hands-on opportunity to think like engineers and problem-solvers, applying real-world design principles to complex exploration scenarios,” said Vemitra Alexander, who leads the Human Exploration Rover Challenge for NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement at Marshall. “By encouraging innovation and teamwork, we’re helping prepare the next generation to contribute to missions that will take us farther into space.”

Here is the full list of winners:

Human-Powered High School Division

First Place: Parish Episcopal School, Dallas, Texas

Second Place: Kealakehe High School, Kailua-Kona, Hawaii

Third Place: Debbie Smith Career and Technical Education Academy, Reno, Nevada

Human-Powered College/University Division

First Place: University of Central Missouri, Warrensburg, Missouri

Second Place: Rhode Island School of Design, Providence, Rhode Island

Third Place: The University of Alabama in Huntsville, Huntsville, Alabama

Remote-Control Middle School/High School Division

First Place: Gould Academy, Bethel, Maine

Second Place: SoulPhamm, South Plainfield, New Jersey

Third Place: Space and Engineering Technologies Academy, San Antonio, Texas

Remote-Control College/University Division

First Place: The University of Alabama in Huntsville, Huntsville, Alabama

Second Place: South Dakota State University, Brookings, South Dakota

Third Place: Florida Atlantic University, Boca Raton, Florida

Rookie of the Year

Gould Academy, Bethel, Maine

Task Challenge Award

Remote-Control Middle School/High School Division: Gould Academy, Bethel, Maine College/University Division: The University of Alabama in Huntsville, Huntsville, Alabama

Human-Powered High School Division: Parish Episcopal School, Dallas, Texas College/University Division: Rhode Island School of Design, Providence, Rhode Island



Ingenuity Award

Queen’s University, Kingston, Ontario, Canada

Phoenix Award

Human-Powered High School Division: Parish Episcopal School, Dallas, Texas College/University Division: Rhode Island School of Design, Providence, Rhode Island

Remote-Control Middle School/High School Division: Gould Academy, Bethel, Maine College/University Division: University of the District of Columbia, Washington, D.C.

Human-Powered High School Division: Parish Episcopal School, Dallas, Texas College/University Division: University of Central Missouri, Warrensburg, Missouri

Remote-Control Middle School/High School Division: SoulPhamm, South Plainfield, New Jersey College/University Division: The University of Alabama in Huntsville, Huntsville, Alabama



Industry STEM Engagement Award

Most Improved Performance Award

Campbell University, Buies Creek, North Carolina

The rover challenge is one of NASA’s eight Artemis Student Challenges reflecting the goals of the Artemis program, which will land Americans on the Moon while establishing a long-term presence for science and exploration, preparing for future human missions to Mars. NASA uses such challenges to encourage students to pursue degrees and careers in the fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

The competition is managed by NASA’s Office of STEM Engagement at NASA Marshall. Since its inception in 1994, more than 15,000 students have participated – with many former students working at NASA, or within the aerospace industry.

Learn more about the Human Exploration Rover Challenge.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's 32nd annual Human Exploration Rover Challenge, a significant educational event that engages over 500 students worldwide in designing and testing lunar rovers. This challenge promotes STEM education, innovation, and prepares the next generation for space exploration missions, representing a meaningful and broad positive impact on society and future space endeavors.
articles.personality_title: NASA crowns winners of 32nd Human Exploration Rover Challenge
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA holds an annual competition called the Human Exploration Rover Challenge. It invites student teams worldwide to design and build rovers that can be powered by people or controlled remotely. This event helps students learn about science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM).

**What happened** – From April 10–11, 2024, over 500 students from 42 teams gathered at the U.S. Space & Rocket Center in Huntsville, Alabama, to race their rovers on a half-mile obstacle course. Teams completed mission tasks and passed safety and design tests. Winners were announced for different age groups and rover types, including high schools and universities.

**Impact** – This challenge gives students a chance to solve real engineering problems and work in teams. It connects education with NASA’s Artemis program, which aims to return humans to the Moon and explore Mars. Many past participants have gone on to work in space and science careers, showing how the challenge helps prepare future explorers.

**What's next step** – NASA will continue to run this challenge each year, encouraging more students to join and improve their skills. The knowledge gained here supports NASA’s long-term space missions and helps grow the STEM workforce needed for future exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s 32nd Human Exploration Rover Challenge brought together hundreds of students worldwide to build and race lunar rovers, inspiring the next generation of space explorers through hands-on STEM learning.
articles.personality_title_fr: NASA annonce les gagnants du 32e défi annuel des rovers d'exploration humaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – NASA organise chaque année un concours appelé le défi des rovers d'exploration humaine. Des équipes d'étudiants du monde entier conçoivent et construisent des rovers actionnés par des personnes ou télécommandés. Cet événement aide les élèves à apprendre les sciences, la technologie, l'ingénierie et les mathématiques (STEM).

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les 10 et 11 avril 2024, plus de 500 étudiants de 42 équipes se sont réunis au U.S. Space & Rocket Center à Huntsville, Alabama, pour faire courir leurs rovers sur un parcours d'obstacles d'un demi-mile. Les équipes ont réalisé des missions et réussi des tests de sécurité et de conception. Les gagnants ont été annoncés selon les catégories d'âge et de type de rover.

**Impact** – Ce défi offre aux étudiants l'occasion de résoudre de vrais problèmes d'ingénierie et de travailler en équipe. Il est lié au programme Artemis de la NASA, qui vise à renvoyer des humains sur la Lune et à explorer Mars. Beaucoup d'anciens participants travaillent aujourd'hui dans le domaine spatial, montrant l'importance de ce défi pour préparer les futurs explorateurs.

**Prochaine étape** – NASA continuera à organiser ce défi chaque année pour encourager plus d'étudiants à participer et à développer leurs compétences. Les connaissances acquises soutiennent les missions spatiales à long terme et renforcent la main-d'œuvre STEM nécessaire pour l'exploration future.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le 32e défi des rovers d'exploration humaine de la NASA a rassemblé des centaines d'étudiants du monde entier pour construire et piloter des rovers lunaires, inspirant la prochaine génération d'explorateurs grâce à un apprentissage pratique en STEM.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA anuncia ganadores del 32º Desafío Anual de Rovers de Exploración Humana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – NASA organiza cada año un concurso llamado Desafío de Rovers de Exploración Humana. Equipos de estudiantes de todo el mundo diseñan y construyen rovers que pueden ser impulsados por personas o controlados a distancia. Este evento ayuda a los estudiantes a aprender ciencias, tecnología, ingeniería y matemáticas (STEM).

**Qué pasó** – Del 10 al 11 de abril de 2024, más de 500 estudiantes de 42 equipos se reunieron en el U.S. Space & Rocket Center en Huntsville, Alabama, para competir con sus rovers en un recorrido de obstáculos de medio milla. Los equipos completaron tareas de misión y aprobaron pruebas de seguridad y diseño. Se anunciaron ganadores en diferentes categorías de edad y tipo de rover.

**Impacto** – Este desafío brinda a los estudiantes la oportunidad de resolver problemas reales de ingeniería y trabajar en equipo. Está vinculado con el programa Artemis de NASA, que busca llevar humanos nuevamente a la Luna y explorar Marte. Muchos participantes anteriores trabajan ahora en la industria espacial, mostrando cómo el desafío prepara a futuros exploradores.

**Próximo paso** – NASA seguirá organizando este desafío cada año para motivar a más estudiantes a participar y mejorar sus habilidades. El conocimiento adquirido apoya las misiones espaciales a largo plazo y fortalece la fuerza laboral STEM necesaria para la exploración futura.

**Resumen en una frase** – El 32º Desafío de Rovers de Exploración Humana de NASA reunió a cientos de estudiantes de todo el mundo para construir y conducir rovers lunares, inspirando a la próxima generación de exploradores mediante el aprendizaje práctico en STEM.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-colored painting of a diverse group of intricately designed lunar rovers—some human-powered with visible pedals and others remote-controlled with antennas—navigating a rugged, rocky obstacle course inspired by the Moon’s surface, set against a softly glowing backdrop of the U.S. Space & Rocket Center’s iconic rocket silhouette under a calm sky.

articles.title: Artemis II Astronauts Aboard USS John P. Murtha
articles.summary: The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman (commander), Christina Koch (mission specialist), Victor Glover (pilot), and CSA astronaut Jeremy Hansen (mission specialist) pose for a group photo in the well […]
articles.content:
NASA/Bill Ingalls

The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight.

NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman (commander), Christina Koch (mission specialist), Victor Glover (pilot), and CSA astronaut Jeremy Hansen (mission specialist) pose for a group photo in the well deck of the USS John P. Murtha after inspecting the Orion spacecraft on Saturday, April 11, 2026.

The crew splashed down in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California on Friday, April 10, at 5:07 p.m. PDT (8:07 p.m. EDT), marking the completion of their Artemis II mission.

After splashdown in the Pacific Ocean, the astronauts were met by a combined NASA and U.S. military team that assisted them out of the spacecraft in open water and transported them via helicopter to the USS John P. Murtha for initial medical checkouts.

Artemis II is the first crewed flight aboard NASA’s human deep space capabilities, paving the way for future lunar surface missions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the successful completion of NASA's Artemis II mission, the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years, marking a significant milestone in human space exploration with broad inspirational and scientific impact.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II astronauts safely return to Earth after historic Moon mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Artemis II mission is the first crewed trip to the Moon in over 50 years. It is a test flight to prepare for future missions where astronauts will land on the Moon’s surface.

**What happened** – On April 10, 2026, four astronauts—Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, Victor Glover, and Jeremy Hansen—returned safely to Earth after traveling to the Moon. They landed in the Pacific Ocean near California and were helped out of their spacecraft by a team from NASA and the U.S. military. They were then taken to the USS John P. Murtha ship for medical checks.

**Impact** – This mission showed that NASA’s new spacecraft, Orion, and its systems work well with people on board during deep space travel. It is an important step toward sending astronauts back to the Moon’s surface and eventually to Mars. The success brings new knowledge and confidence for future space exploration.

**What's next step** – NASA will study the data from Artemis II to improve spacecraft and mission plans. The next missions will focus on landing astronauts on the Moon and building a long-term presence there.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission successfully tested human travel to the Moon, opening the door for future lunar exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d’Artemis II reviennent sur Terre après une mission lunaire historique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA est le premier voyage habité vers la Lune depuis plus de 50 ans. C’est un vol d’essai pour préparer de futures missions avec des astronautes sur la surface lunaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 avril 2026, quatre astronautes—Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, Victor Glover et Jeremy Hansen—sont revenus sur Terre en toute sécurité après leur voyage vers la Lune. Ils ont atterri dans l’océan Pacifique près de la Californie et ont été aidés à sortir de leur vaisseau spatial par une équipe de la NASA et de l’armée américaine. Ils ont ensuite été transportés vers le navire USS John P. Murtha pour des contrôles médicaux.

**Impact** – Cette mission a montré que le nouveau vaisseau spatial Orion de la NASA et ses systèmes fonctionnent bien avec des humains à bord lors d’un voyage dans l’espace lointain. C’est une étape importante pour envoyer des astronautes sur la surface de la Lune et plus tard vers Mars. Le succès apporte de nouvelles connaissances et de la confiance pour l’exploration spatiale future.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA étudiera les données d’Artemis II pour améliorer le vaisseau spatial et les plans de mission. Les prochaines missions viseront à faire atterrir des astronautes sur la Lune et à y construire une présence durable.

**Phrase clé** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA a testé avec succès le voyage humain vers la Lune, ouvrant la voie à l’exploration lunaire future.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II regresan a la Tierra tras una histórica misión lunar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA es el primer viaje tripulado a la Luna en más de 50 años. Es un vuelo de prueba para preparar futuras misiones con astronautas en la superficie lunar.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de abril de 2026, cuatro astronautas—Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, Victor Glover y Jeremy Hansen—regresaron sanos y salvos a la Tierra después de viajar a la Luna. Aterrizaron en el océano Pacífico cerca de California y fueron ayudados a salir de su nave espacial por un equipo de la NASA y el ejército de Estados Unidos. Luego los trasladaron al barco USS John P. Murtha para revisiones médicas.

**Impacto** – Esta misión demostró que la nueva nave espacial Orion de la NASA y sus sistemas funcionan bien con personas a bordo durante el viaje al espacio profundo. Es un paso importante para enviar astronautas a la superficie lunar y eventualmente a Marte. El éxito aporta nuevos conocimientos y confianza para la exploración espacial futura.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA estudiará los datos de Artemis II para mejorar la nave espacial y los planes de misión. Las próximas misiones se centrarán en aterrizar astronautas en la Luna y construir una presencia duradera allí.

**Frase clave** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA probó con éxito el viaje humano a la Luna, abriendo la puerta para futuras exploraciones lunares.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of four stylized astronaut helmets resting together on the deck of a naval ship, with a softly glowing Orion spacecraft silhouette reflected in calm ocean waters nearby under a serene sunset sky.

articles.title: We’ve caught a comet switching its spin direction for the first time
articles.summary: A small comet has been spotted slowing down and then speeding up again – but in the opposite direction, which we have never seen before
articles.content:
A small comet has been spotted slowing down and then speeding up again – but in the opposite direction, which we have never seen before

An artist’s impression of comet 41P as it approached the sun and shot material off into space NASA, ESA, CSA, Ralf Crawford (STScI)

A small comet seems to have switched the direction in which it is rotating – the first time astronomers have seen evidence of such behaviour. Changes like this may help us learn about the insides of comets, which could reveal information about the composition of the early solar system.

Comet 41P/Tuttle-Giacobini-Kresák, or simply 41P, measures about 1 kilometre across and takes around 5.4 years to orbit the sun. We can only see it when it visits the inner solar system and its trajectory happens to take it relatively close to Earth. It was last seen in 2017.

In March that year, it was rotating at a rate of about one full spin every 20 hours. When astronomers observed it just two months later, it had slowed down dramatically to one spin every 46 to 60 hours. Now, David Jewitt at the University of California, Los Angeles, has reanalysed observations from the Hubble Space Telescope taken in December 2017 and found that the comet had sped up again to one spin every 14 hours or so.

The simplest explanation is that the comet’s rotational speed slowed until it eventually reached zero, at which point the comet began to rotate in the opposite direction, picking up rotational speed as it did so. This may be because sunlight caused ice on the comet’s surface to sublimate away into gas that then acted like a jet. If this jet fired in the opposite direction from the comet’s original rotational direction, it would slow down the comet’s rotational speed and eventually send it spinning in the opposite direction.

“It is the first detected ‘fast’ change of the rotation direction for a celestial body,” says Dmitrii Vavilov at the University of Washington in Seattle. Most of the time, significant changes in any celestial body, even such a small comet, take decades or centuries.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

“Following 41P during its next apparition in late 2027/early 2028 will be quite interesting,” says John Noonan at Auburn University in Alabama. “I’d be keen to see if these comets are more likely to fracture as well, due to the stress.” If 41P spins too fast, its main body, or nucleus, will simply fall apart.

“I expect this nucleus will very quickly self-destruct,” said Jewitt in a statement. In fact, it may have already happened. If so, it could present an excellent opportunity to observe the insides of a comet that froze while the solar system was forming. Studying the composition of such ancient ice could not only give us precious insights into the chemical make-up of the early solar system, but also act as a benchmark to work out how that chemistry changed as the solar system matured.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a novel astronomical observation of a comet reversing its spin direction, a first-ever detected phenomenon with significant scientific implications. This discovery can enhance understanding of comet composition and early solar system chemistry, offering broad benefits to space science and humanity's knowledge of cosmic origins.
articles.personality_title: Scientists observe a comet reversing its spin direction for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Comets are icy objects that orbit the sun, often showing changes in speed or direction as they get closer to the sun. These changes usually happen slowly over many years.

**What happened** – Astronomers watched comet 41P/Tuttle-Giacobini-Kresák, about 1 kilometer wide, and saw it slow down its spin from one turn every 20 hours to almost stopping. Then, it started spinning again but in the opposite direction. This was observed in 2017 using the Hubble Space Telescope.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen a comet completely reverse its spin direction so quickly. The change is likely caused by jets of gas pushing the comet as sunlight turns ice into gas. Understanding this helps scientists learn what comets are made of and how they behave, which in turn teaches us about the early solar system when planets were forming.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to watch comet 41P when it returns around 2027 or 2028 to see if it breaks apart due to spinning too fast. If it does, scientists hope to study its inside, which could hold clues about the building blocks of our solar system.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, a comet has been seen to stop and reverse its spin, offering new clues about comet behavior and the early solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques observent pour la première fois une comète inverser sa direction de rotation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les comètes sont des objets glacés qui tournent autour du soleil et qui changent souvent de vitesse ou de direction en s’approchant du soleil. Ces changements se produisent généralement lentement sur plusieurs années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les astronomes ont observé la comète 41P/Tuttle-Giacobini-Kresák, d’environ 1 kilomètre de large, et ont vu sa rotation ralentir, passant d’un tour toutes les 20 heures à presque s’arrêter. Puis elle a recommencé à tourner, mais dans la direction opposée. Cela a été observé en 2017 grâce au télescope spatial Hubble.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques voient une comète inverser complètement sa rotation aussi rapidement. Ce changement est probablement causé par des jets de gaz qui poussent la comète lorsque la lumière du soleil transforme la glace en gaz. Comprendre cela aide à découvrir la composition et le comportement des comètes, ce qui nous renseigne aussi sur le système solaire primitif.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’observer la comète 41P lors de son retour vers 2027-2028 pour voir si elle se brise à cause d’une rotation trop rapide. Si c’est le cas, ils espèrent étudier son intérieur, qui pourrait révéler des indices sur la formation de notre système solaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, une comète a été vue s’arrêter puis inverser sa rotation, offrant de nouvelles informations sur le comportement des comètes et l’histoire du système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos observan por primera vez un cometa que invierte su dirección de giro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los cometas son objetos helados que orbitan alrededor del sol y suelen cambiar su velocidad o dirección al acercarse al sol. Estos cambios normalmente ocurren lentamente durante muchos años.

**Qué pasó** – Los astrónomos observaron el cometa 41P/Tuttle-Giacobini-Kresák, de aproximadamente 1 kilómetro de ancho, y vieron que su giro se ralentizó, pasando de una vuelta cada 20 horas a casi detenerse. Luego, comenzó a girar de nuevo, pero en la dirección opuesta. Esto se observó en 2017 con el telescopio espacial Hubble.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven a un cometa invertir completamente su giro tan rápido. El cambio probablemente fue causado por chorros de gas que empujan al cometa cuando la luz solar convierte el hielo en gas. Entender esto ayuda a conocer de qué están hechos los cometas y cómo actúan, lo que también nos enseña sobre el sistema solar temprano.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean observar al cometa 41P cuando regrese alrededor de 2027 o 2028 para ver si se rompe por girar demasiado rápido. Si eso pasa, esperan estudiar su interior, que podría contener pistas sobre la formación de nuestro sistema solar.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, se ha visto a un cometa detenerse y cambiar su giro, ofreciendo nuevas pistas sobre el comportamiento de los cometas y el sistema solar temprano.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a small, glowing comet with swirling icy jets reversing direction around its nucleus, set against a softly glowing sunlit space background, using warm, natural tones to highlight the dynamic spin reversal and delicate sublimation of ice into gas.
